title: Calystegia macrostegia
intro: Calystegia macrostegia, also known as Island False Bindweed, is a perennial vine native to the Channel Islands of California. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine, offering potential health benefits such as anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of anxiety and insomnia. The bioactive constituents of Calystegia macrostegia include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Calystegia macrostegia is often used in herbal preparations, including teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are commonly used to promote relaxation and reduce stress.
taxonomy: Calystegia macrostegia is a species of morning glory vine, classified within the family Convolvulaceae. This family is a group of flowering plants commonly known as bindweeds or morning glories. The genus Calystegia is a group of flowering plants that are often considered to be a subset of the more widely distributed genus Ipomoea. However, the species Calystegia macrostegia is generally considered to be a member of the genus Calystegia due to its unique characteristics and distribution. The classification of Calystegia macrostegia is further supported by its distinct morphology and phylogenetic relationships.

side_effects: Calystegia macrostegia, also known as Island Morning Glory, can cause skin and eye irritation if not handled properly. Its sap may cause dermatitis or allergic reactions in some individuals. Prolonged exposure to its milky sap may also lead to gastrointestinal issues, such as nausea and diarrhea.
